channel.title: Contract on Detention Pipeline
channel.description: Recent content in Contract on Detention Pipeline

item.title: BI Incorporated — ISAP V IDIQ Contract (Ankle Monitors & Surveillance)
item.description:
BI Incorporated — ISAP V Contract

[[bi-incorporated|BI Incorporated]], a subsidiary of [[geo-group|The GEO Group]], holds the sole-source contract for ICE’s Intensive Supervision Appearance Program (ISAP) – the federal government’s primary immigration surveillance program, marketed as “alternatives to detention.”

Contract History

Contract Period Value
ISAP (early iterations) Pre-2020 Various
ISAP IV 2020-2025 $2.2 billion
ISAP V Oct 1, 2025 — (1+1 year) Up to $1 billion
Total projected through 2026 $700M+ in surveillance revenue

What the Contract Covers

Under ISAP V, BI provides:

item.title: BI Incorporated — Skip Tracing Contract ($121M)
item.description:
BI Incorporated — Skip Tracing Contract

In December 2025, ICE awarded [[bi-incorporated|BI Incorporated]] (a [[geo-group|GEO Group]] subsidiary) a two-year contract worth up to $121 million for “skip tracing” services – using surveillance data to locate people who have missed check-ins so ICE can arrest them.

What This Means

This contract transforms BI from a monitoring company into an active enforcement participant. The company that tracks immigrants through ankle monitors and the SmartLINK app now also hunts them down when they stop complying. The data collected under the [[bi-incorporated-isap-v|ISAP V monitoring contract]] feeds directly into skip-tracing operations.

item.title: Bradford County FL — Sabot Consulting IGSA Proposal ($239M/year at full capacity)
item.description:
Bradford County IGSA Proposal

Contract Structure

The [[sabot-consulting]] briefing package (December 16, 2025) describes a county-led IGSA in which:

  1. Bradford County retains ownership of the land and existing warehouse
  2. A prime contractor/operator (not yet selected) builds and operates the facility
  3. ICE pays per-bed-day rates
  4. [[sabot-consulting]] provides “Office of ICE Integration” support — compliance, communications, political management
  5. Bradford County Sheriff’s Office provides governance and oversight

Financial Model

Phase Beds Monthly Annual Per-Bed-Day County Recovery
1 1,000 $8.08M $96.9M $269 $80K/month
2 2,000 $14.2M $170.2M $236 $142K/month
3 3,000 $19.9M $239M $221 $199K/month

Mobilization costs: $8.9M (Phase 1), $6.3M (Phase 2), $6.2M (Phase 3) — payable upfront or amortized.

item.title: Camp East Montana — $1.2B Army Contract for ICE Tent Detention Camp
item.description:
Camp East Montana $1.2B Contract

Overview

A $1.2 billion contract awarded through the U.S. Army’s procurement system to build and operate a tent detention camp at [[camp-east-montana-fort-bliss]] on Fort Bliss, El Paso, TX. The contractor was a company registered at a residential address in Virginia with no prior detention experience.

Why This Contract Matters

  1. Military procurement bypass: Awarded through DOD, not DHS, avoiding standard ICE contracting oversight
  2. No-experience contractor: The company had no track record in detention operations
  3. Scale: $1.2B for a single tent camp — compare to the entire Bradford County proposal at $239M/year
  4. Outcome: Three deaths within the facility’s first five months, including one ruled homicide by the El Paso County Medical Examiner. TB cases documented. Dozens of detention standard violations at opening.

The Contractor Problem

The mystery surrounding the contractor was first reported by PBS News. A company registered at a residential Virginia address — with no visible history of detention operations — received over a billion dollars to build and operate what became the most notorious facility in the new detention system.

item.title: Clearview AI — $9.2M ICE facial recognition contract (Sept 2025)
item.description:
Clearview AI — ICE Facial Recognition Contract (2025)

In September 2025, ICE’s Homeland Security Investigations (HSI) finalized a $9.2 million sole-source contract with [[clearview-ai|Clearview AI]] for biometric facial matching software.

Contract Structure

  • Base period: One year
  • Options: Three one-year extensions
  • Total potential value: $9.2 million

Stated Purpose

The contract provides facial recognition for two investigative missions:

  1. Child sexual exploitation cases
  2. Investigations into assaults on law enforcement officers

However, once deployed, the technology’s actual use is difficult to oversee or constrain to stated purposes.

item.title: G4S Secure Solutions — ICE Ground Transportation & Custody Contracts
item.description:
G4S Secure Solutions — ICE Ground Transportation

[[g4s-secure-solutions|G4S Secure Solutions]] (Allied Universal subsidiary) provides armed ground transportation and detention officer services across multiple ICE field offices under a series of task orders and delivery contracts.

Known Contracts

Field Office Value Status
San Antonio TBD Active
Los Angeles TBD Extended Sep 2025 (3 months)
Phoenix TBD Active
San Francisco TBD Extended Sep 2025 (3 months)
Salt Lake City $10.4M Awarded Mar 2026
San Diego Up to $100M Solicited Dec 2025; award Q2 FY2026

Services Under Contract

  • Armed transportation of detainees in government-owned and contractor-owned vehicles
  • Detention officer staffing for custody during transport
  • Courtroom guarding
  • 24-hour, year-round operations with trained, uniformed detention officers

Contract Structure

The San Diego solicitation reveals the structure: firm-fixed-price, indefinite-delivery/indefinite-quantity (IDIQ) multiple-award contracts, set aside for small businesses, running through January 14, 2031.

item.title: GEO Group — WEXMAC-TITUS Pre-Qualification (No-Bid Access to $45B)
item.description:
GEO Group WEXMAC-TITUS Pre-Qualification

The Mechanism

On a Friday night in February 2026, the Trump administration quietly announced that [[geo-group]] had been added to the [[wexmac-titus-military-procurement-bypass|WEXMAC-TITUS]] list of pre-qualified vendors. This gives GEO Group no-bid access to the $45 billion OBBBA detention funding pool — the ability to receive task orders for warehouse construction, facility operation, and detention services without competitive bidding.

Other Pre-Qualified Vendors

Companies added alongside GEO specialize in:

item.title: Glades County FL — Reinstated IGSA (up to 500 beds, April 2025)
item.description:
Glades County IGSA

ICE reinstated the Intergovernmental Service Agreement with [[glades-county-fl-detention]] on April 3, 2025, for up to 500 beds. The facility is operated by the Glades County Sheriff’s Office. [[sabot-consulting]] featured Glades County as a “Client Profile” in their newsletter, indicating Sabot’s involvement in the IGSA relationship.

This is one of the existing Florida sheriff-operated IGSA facilities (alongside [[baker-county-fl-detention]]) that the Bradford County proposal explicitly cites as models: “Other counties in Florida — including Baker County and Glades County — whose sheriff’s offices already operate ICE detention facilities in partnership with the federal government.”

item.title: ICE Air Operations — CSI Aviation Charter Flight Contract (FY2025-2026)
item.description:
ICE Air Operations — CSI Aviation Charter Flight Contract

ICE pays [[csi-aviation|CSI Aviation]] to manage the entire ICE Air charter flight program, including both international deportation flights and domestic detainee transfers. CSI then subcontracts to airlines including [[globalx-airlines|GlobalX]], [[world-atlantic-airways|World Atlantic Airways]], and others.

Contract Values

The ICE Air program has scaled dramatically under Trump’s second term:

Period Obligated Amount
FY2024 (Biden) $363.9M
FY2025 (Trump) $1.1B
FY2026 (first 5 months) $673.4M
FY2026 potential ceiling $1.5B

Key Sub-Contracts

  • March 2025 no-bid: Up to $219 million, March-August 2025 (extendable to February 2026), for removal flights
  • FY2025 daily charter: $562 million for daily charter flights including scheduled large aircraft and high-risk transport

Flight Volume (2025-2026)

  • 14,426 enforcement flights in the reporting period, up 89% year-over-year
  • Deportation flights: Increased 46%
  • Domestic transfer flights: Surged 132%, from 3,909 to 9,066 flights – the single largest category of growth

The domestic transfer surge is critical context: as ICE opens new detention facilities in remote locations (Montana, Louisiana, Texas), more flights are needed to move detainees through a geographically dispersed system. The flight budget is not just about deportation – it is the connective tissue of the detention infrastructure.

item.title: ICE Skip Tracing Program — $1.2B across 13 contractors (Dec 2025)
item.description:
ICE Skip Tracing Program (2025)

In November 2025, ICE launched a program to contract private-sector “skip tracing” services to locate immigrants on ICE’s docket. In December 2025, ICE awarded contracts to 13 private companies with a combined potential value of $1.2 billion over two years, targeting up to 1.5 million immigrants.

How It Works

Contractors receive personal data on targets – names, dates of birth, addresses, contact information. Each company may receive up to 50,000 cases per month. The process:

item.title: LexisNexis LEIDS — $22.1M ICE + $15.9M CBP database subscription
item.description:
LexisNexis LEIDS Contracts

[[lexisnexis|LexisNexis]] provides ICE and CBP with the Law Enforcement Investigative Database Subscription (LEIDS) platform, a web-based investigative database that includes license plate recognition.

Contract Values

  • ICE contract: Up to $22.1 million, running through potentially 2026
  • CBP contract: Up to $15.9 million, running through potentially 2027
  • Combined: Up to $38 million

What LEIDS Provides

The LEIDS platform provides access to the Accurint database, which aggregates 10,000+ data sources to create encyclopedic dossiers on individuals including names, addresses, phone numbers, employment history, vehicle registration, property records, utility records, and license plate recognition data.

item.title: MVM Inc. — ICE Detainee Transportation Contract ($1.1B cumulative)
item.description:
MVM Inc. — ICE Transportation Contract

[[mvm-inc|MVM, Inc.]] holds one of the largest cumulative ICE contract portfolios for detainee transportation, with $1.1 billion in total obligated contracts and $145.1 million in new 2025 contracts alone.

Scope of Services

MVM’s transportation contract covers two distinct populations:

Adult Detainee Transportation

  • Ground and air transport of ICE detainees between facilities
  • Escort services during transfers
  • Support for ERO enforcement operations

Unaccompanied Children Transportation

  • Transport of unaccompanied migrant children from CBP custody to HHS/ORR shelters
  • Commercial flight escorts for minors
  • Family unit transfers
  • Original UAC contract (2014): $162 million, later expanded to $128 million in additional obligations

Political Connections

The Marquez family, which owns MVM, has been identified by OpenSecrets as major Trump donors who are “reaping billions in ICE contracts.” Kevin Marquez purchased the company from his father Dario Marquez Jr. in 2015.

item.title: Palantir ICM (Investigative Case Management) — $139.3M (2022-2026)
item.description:
Palantir ICM Contract (2022)

ICE’s Investigative Case Management (ICM) system is built and operated by [[palantir-technologies|Palantir Technologies]]. The current contract was awarded in 2022, valued at $139.3 million, and set to expire in April 2026.

System Capabilities

ICM is the official record system for ICE investigations. It:

  • Logs and manages criminal and civil investigation files
  • Structures case workflows
  • Documents investigative activity
  • Integrates with ICE Enforcement and Removal Operations (ERO) deportation records

Sole-Source Renewal

ICE has indicated the contract is slated for sole-source renewal because the agency cannot find another company that makes a comparable system. This pattern – building critical infrastructure on a proprietary platform, then citing sole-source justification for renewals – effectively locks ICE into permanent dependency on Palantir.

item.title: Palantir ImmigrationOS — $30M prototype + $29.9M maintenance (2025-2027)
item.description:
Palantir ImmigrationOS Contracts (2025)

Prototype Development Contract — $30M

In April 2025, ICE awarded Palantir a $30 million contract to develop the Immigration Lifecycle Operating System (ImmigrationOS). Prototype delivery was required by September 25, 2025, with full operational capability targeted for September 2027.

ImmigrationOS is designed to manage the entire deportation pipeline:

  • Targeting and enforcement prioritization (who to deport first)
  • Self-deportation tracking
  • Immigration lifecycle management (identification through removal)

The system includes the ELITE (Enhanced Leads Identification & Targeting for Enforcement) app, which maps deportation targets on a digital map with dossiers and “confidence scores” for address accuracy.

item.title: Thomson Reuters CLEAR — $22.8M DHS/ICE database subscription (through 2026)
item.description:
Thomson Reuters CLEAR — ICE Database Subscription

Primary Contract — $22.8M

[[thomson-reuters|Thomson Reuters Special Services]] holds a five-year, $22.8 million contract with DHS for ICE’s use of a “law enforcement investigative database subscription.” The 2025 option year explicitly notes “provision of license plate reader data,” confirming CLEAR provides ICE with ALPR data in addition to personal records.

West Publishing Contract — $1.7M + $1.9M

West Publishing, a Thomson Reuters subsidiary, entered a $1.7 million contract with DHS for ICE CLEAR access in April 2023. A $1.9 million option was exercised in March 2025.

item.title: Vigilant Solutions (Motorola) — $22.8M ICE ALPR database access (through 2026)
item.description:
Vigilant Solutions ALPR Database — ICE Contract

ICE holds a $22.8 million contract for access to the [[vigilant-solutions-motorola|Vigilant Solutions]] automated license plate reader (ALPR) database, running through 2026. The database contains billions of license plate location records from commercial and law enforcement sources.

Additional Hardware Purchase (2025)

ICE also issued a purchase order for four mobile license plate reader units (~$95,500) to support Enforcement and Removal Operations (ERO), indicating ICE is deploying its own ALPR hardware in addition to accessing the shared database.
